data.title: Powell Industries: Temporary Slowdown Creates An Attractive Entry Point
data.description:
The Thesis

Exiting the first half of FY25, Powell Industries (NASDAQ:POWL) delivered another quarter showing moderating topline growth, as the petrochemical and the oil and gas revenue declined year-on-year. However, moving ahead, I expect continued momentum across other markets, including utilities and the commercial and other industrials, and elevated backlog levels near $1.3 billion should help the company deliver steady growth across the consolidated topline in the rest of FY25. For margins, I think POWL's focus on excellent project execution, higher operational efficiency, and favorable backlog mix with high-margin projects should be key growth drivers in the quarters ahead. The stock has been down in mid-teen percentages since my last bullish article. However, as the prospects remain favorable in the near term despite a slowdown in the core end markets, the company's intact long-term outlook and an attractive valuation multiple make POWL a compelling buy at the current levels.

Q2 FY25 Highlights

Throughout 2024, POWL delivered robust double-digit growth across its consolidated topline, mainly driven by some large-scale projects during FY23. However, entering into FY25, the overall topline growth started to moderate as certain large-scale projects, booked in FY23 in the company’s core industrial end market, inched towards completion. While the revenue contributions from these projects were significant during FY24, the company reported a year-on-year decline of approximately 13% and 3% in its petrochemical and oil and gas sectors, respectively, across its core industrial end markets in Q2 FY25.

However, the electricity and the commercial and other industrial sectors continue to show strengths, delivering double-digit growth. As a result, despite weakness in core markets, the company’s consolidated topline grew about 9.2% versus the prior year quarter to $278.6 million in the second quarter of FY25.

Although the topline growth slightly moderated during the quarter, the company’s strong project and operation improvements during the quarter benefited the margins notably, as its operating margin expanded to a record 21.1% from about 15.5% in the same quarter of FY24. Higher operating income also benefited the company’s bottom line performance as its adjusted EPS jumped to $3.81 from $2.75 a year ago, beating the estimates by a decent $0.24 in the second quarter of FY25.

Outlook

After a solid FY24 with nearly 50% growth in most of the quarters, POWL’s topline growth started to moderate as it entered into FY25, primarily due to normalizing order trends and tougher comparison from robust growth in the core end markets, including petrochemical and oil and gas. Going ahead, while I expect tougher comps to impact the company’s revenue growth in the core end markets in the rest of FY25, the demand environment remains healthy for the company’s equipment and solutions. As a result, the company booked two new large-scale projects, of which one is in the oil and gas sector, for a new Greenfield LNG facility in the U.S. Gulf Coast, which should support the end market revenue in the quarters ahead.

Apart from this, the demand environment in the utility end market also remains strong due to continuing robust project activity, which has also resulted in market share gains in the past few quarters. As a result, the utilities end market now accounts for approximately 23% share in total revenue, up from 19% in FY24. In my view, this end market should continue to contribute meaningfully to the overall volume growth in FY25. Moving to the commercial and other industrial end market, which includes the data center market, also continues to show strength as project activity remains healthy, primarily within the data center and related infrastructure, with no signs of change or slowdown, further supporting volumes in the quarters ahead.

In addition, the company’s backlog also remains elevated at nearly $1.3 billion. While the growth in backlog has been flat in the past few quarters due to normalizing orders, it remains strong, giving solid visibility for revenue in FY25 and beyond. Overall, I believe the consolidated topline growth should remain under pressure due to tougher comps and normalizing order growth in the core end markets of petrochemical and oil and gas, due to its significant 53% share in revenue. However, the continued robust demand environment in other end markets related to utilities and data centers, along with robust backlog levels, should continue to support overall topline growth in 2025.

Although POWL looks well positioned to continue to deliver steady growth across the topline in the quarters ahead, its continued investment in R&D of new products to enhance its product portfolio with evolving customer needs should support growth in the longer term. In the last quarter, the company commercially launched multiple new and innovative products, including a ground switch, primarily focused on industrial markets like oil and gas, and a new compact substation to optimize workspace and provide environmental protection for utilities.

In addition to these, the company also showcased the first design of its data center and associated market-specific, low-voltage switchgear product, which should fuel demand in the longer term. POWL has also just completed its capacity expansion at its Houston electrical product facility, which should help deal with rising demand and backlog conversion in the coming years. The facility is set to ramp up manufacturing of high-value products like the station breaker, which was launched last year, and the new power control substation in the upcoming quarter, which should contribute to the revenue in the latter half of FY25.

In addition to this, the new facility is also expected to help advance the company’s key strategic priority of commercializing new products by investing in in-house innovation rather than M&As. In my view, while the expanded capacity is expected to support higher demand for its products in the coming years, I believe this along with a focus on portfolio diversification should also enhance POWL’s competitiveness across major sectors like utility, commercial and industrial, and oil & gas due to the expansion of next-gen electrical infrastructure solutions tailored for modern utility and industrial needs, driving market share gain and topline growth in the coming years.

Valuation Update

I last covered POWL earlier this year in February, post Q1'FY25 results, maintaining my buy rating due to favorable prospects and an attractive discount after the recent decline in stock price amid a sell-off across tech and AI-related stocks, followed by the DeepSeek shock. However, since then, the stock has been very volatile, mainly due to concerns around its slowing growth, which, along with the recent tariffs-driven sell-off, dragged the stock close to its 52-week low in March, completely wiping out the past year's gains. While the stock has slightly recovered in the past few weeks, along with the recovery across the broader market, the stock saw another sharp decline after its Q2'FY25 results, missing topline estimates. As a result, the stock is still about 17% lower since my last article, against a nearly 7% decline in the S&P 500 during the same period.

Currently, the company's stock is trading at a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 12.29, based on its FY25 (ending Sep'25) EPS estimate of $14.20. While the recent POWL's stock performance has been disappointing, it continues to trade at an attractive discount to its historical multiple, considering its strong double-digit forward growth estimates, as can be seen in the table below..

I expect the robust growth momentum in the utility and the data center-related end market to continue further as the demand environment and project activity remain healthy. However, expected moderation in the core petrochemical and the oil and gas sector revenue growth should impact the overall topline growth in FY25 due to its significant share in the total revenue. But, despite slowing growth across the topline, POWL's continued focus on excellent project execution and improving operational efficiency should enhance its profitability in the quarters ahead. In addition to this, the company's backlog also includes several high-margin large-scale projects, which should further help the company in its margin expansion in FY25 and beyond. And with a debt-free balance sheet and no interest burden, I think these margin gains should translate directly into bottom-line growth, further enhancing the stock valuation in the future.

Risks

Similar to my previous article, the key concern for POWL's growth remains its moderating topline growth due to slight softness in the company's core end markets. My concern also materialized, as the company's stock dropped significantly after a quarter with disappointing topline growth, missing the estimates. While this concern appears to be priced into the stock now, the execution risk remains. If the company could not perform as per expectations or deliver another miss in the next quarter's earnings, investors' sentiment could weaken further, and the POWL stock might see another pullback in the future.

Final Verdict

As discussed above, the stock is currently at an attractive discount versus its historical levels. The company's topline is expected to continue to expand steadily in the quarters ahead due to robust backlog and healthy demand in most of the end markets. Anticipated volume growth, along with better operational performance and benefit from the high margin backlog, should drive margins in FY25 and beyond. The company's long-term also remains intact, with a focus on diversification and new product development. Considering these factors and an extremely discounted valuation, I am still bullish on the stock. Therefore, I would like to give POWL another buy rating at the current levels for investors with a long-term view.

data.title: Productivity And The Growth Question
data.description:
We sometimes refer to productivity as “the most important macroeconomic data point that nobody pays attention to.” That’s in contrast to the holy trinity of jobs, inflation and GDP growth, the three reports that generate the most chatter among financial media types.

The productivity report for the first quarter came out yesterday, but unless you were deep into the economics pages of the Financial Times or the Wall Street Journal, its coming and going would have passed you by.

That’s a pity because in the long run our economic prosperity depends on growth, and in our demographically challenged times, the only path to sustainable growth runs through productivity.

The population growth rate is in decline, and with a growing ratio of deaths to births, the percentage of the population in peak working age years is declining. Either we grow through increased productivity, or we don’t grow – it’s that simple.

With that in mind, it wasn’t great to see productivity decline in the first quarter by a greater than expected minus 0.8 percent (annualized) from last year’s fourth quarter, marking the first quarter of negative growth since 2022. Let’s take a big picture look at how we got from earlier economic cycles to where we are today.

Making More with Less

There are several technical definitions for productivity, but the underlying concept is always the same: making more stuff with less expenditure of effort, time and money. The importance of this metric was one of the key insights Adam Smith described in his seminal 1776 tract “The Wealth of Nations,” explaining how a pin factory could massively increase its daily output through the implementation of specialized labor processes.

That was in the earliest years of the Industrial Revolution; one hundred years later, the manufacturing enterprises of Great Britain and the United States were achieving productivity gains at a rate commensurate with Smith’s pin factory on steroids.

Productivity in the US continued to grow at a brisk clip through much of the twentieth century. After the Second World War, the Bureau of Labor Statistics started measuring productivity gains, and through the first quarter century or so after the war, the average rate of productivity growth from quarter to quarter was around 2.7 percent (annualized). US manufacturing dominated the world, and organizations learned how to optimize their production processes at a scale that would have been previously unimaginable.

Productivity growth started to fall off sharply in the early 1970s, though, and apart from a brief resurgence in the early 2000s (largely seen as the impact of Information Age innovations), it has lagged ever since.

The chart below shows that from 2021 to the present, the average growth rate was just 1.09 percent. That is more than two and a half times less than that 2.7 percent growth rate from 1947 to 1973.

Is AI the Answer?

What’s the recipe for improving productivity? Opinions vary among economists. There is one school, perhaps best represented by Northwestern University economist Robert Gordon, that deems it unlikely we will ever see the kind of productivity growth we enjoyed in the middle years of the last century.

In his book “The Rise and Fall of American Growth” Gordon argues that the productive power we got from the critical inventions of electricity and the internal combustion engine are unlikely to ever be repeated, and we’ve already squeezed as much as we can out of those.

Others, however, point to the observed fact that there is usually a time lag between a new invention and that invention’s translation to commercial gain. Consider electricity; it was invented in 1879, but in 1919, forty years later, only half of American houses were connected to the grid. More recently, semiconductor chips had been around for more than forty years before we saw that brief Information Age productivity surge in the early-mid 2000s.

With this in mind, perhaps it is not surprising that many of the productivity optimists among us today point to artificial intelligence. After all, it has been a scant two years since ChatGPT burst onto the scene, launching a public fascination with AI and, of course, the stock market craze that drove much of the upside in those two back-to-back 20 percent-plus years on the S&P 500 in 2023-24.

We know that, despite all the excited chatter, less than ten percent of all businesses claim to have AI-driven processes deep into their business models. Perhaps it will just take a bit more time before the effects show up in the productivity numbers. It would be nice to think so because the growth isn’t going to happen all by itself – and it isn’t going to happen from whatever is passing for economic policymaking in Washington these days.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ATI: Poised For Robust Growth As Well As Tariff Challenges
data.description:
Investment thesis

Shares of ATI Inc. (NYSE:ATI) have popped in recent days, the result of reduced labor uncertainty and a robust earnings report.

It’s quite possible the share price will keep rising, based on anticipated earnings growth. While there’s uncertainty about tariffs, management has taken steps to reduce future volatility and it has a strong backlog.

I have a one-year price target that is 21.1% higher than the May 7 closing price, and rate it a Buy.

About ATI

The company produces specialty materials, primarily for aerospace and defense markets. These markets accounted for about 62% of total sales in 2024, according to its 10-K for 2024. The following slide from its Q1-2025 earnings call presentation provides additional information about its specialty:

It also produces specialty materials for other markets: specialty energy, medical, and electronics. Operations go through two segments: High Performance Materials & Components [HPMC] and Advanced Alloys & Solutions [AA&S]. In 2024, HPMC accounted for 52% of total revenue and AA&S contributed 48%.

Investors should note that some of its raw materials come from foreign sources. For example, it imports nickel from Canada, Norway, Japan, Finland, and South Africa. And it brings in multiple products from China. Tariffs may materially increase its input costs, although the firm says it has a diverse and flexible supply chain.

ATI also has an international manufacturing and sales profile; according to the 10-K, non-domestic sales made up about 42% of total sales in 2024. So, its international sales also may be affected by tariffs.

At the close on May 8, 2025, shares traded at $71.10, and it had a market cap of $9.84 billion.

Competition and competitive advantages

The 10-K lists numerous public and private competitors for each of its segments, so we have to assume competition is likely high. Further, this competition involves sophisticated metal sciences, so we also should assume innovation and product development matter.

It names two public competitors: Howmet Aerospace Inc. (HWM) and Carpenter Technology Corporation (CRS).

Among its claimed competitive advantages are its research, development, and technical services. To assess that claim and its other competitive strengths, we will compare its net income margin with those of Howmet and Carpenter. This is the net margin, 10-year chart:

Since the third quarter of 2023, ATI’s net margin has slipped, while those of Howmet and Carpenter rose. Note, too, how much of a dip the COVID-19 supply-chain problems had on ATI compared to its rivals.

The same story is playing out for return on equity or ROE. After peaking in Q3-2023, it has slipped back below Howmet again:

Based on the information in these charts, I believe ATI has a medium moat.

First quarter results

ATI released its Q1-2025 earnings report on May 1, 2025. Highlights included:

  • Sales up 10.0% year-over-year, mostly driven by an increase of 23% in aerospace and defense.
  • Net income grew 47%, y-o-y.
  • EPS jumped 45.56% when compared to Q1-2024.

Here are details from a slide in the earnings call presentation:

While Q2-Q2 comparisons are positive, also look at the consecutive quarter comparisons on the right side of the table as well.

On the earnings call, President and CEO Kim Fields made an optimistic case, first in terms of what the company is doing, and second in how it is insulating itself from tariffs and their effects.

She said a couple of times that Q1 was strong, and that Q1 continued the strong momentum ATI built up in Q4-2024. According to Fields, robust demand continued in the key aerospace and defense markets. In addition, “Customers continue to turn to ATI for our differentiated products, recognizing us as a critical supplier in their value chain. Our ability to deliver high quality consistently at scale has led to expansion of long-term contracts and increased share positions across key platforms.”

On the trade and tariff front, Fields said ATI is “uniquely positioned” to make its way through the current uncertainty. The majority of its production takes place domestically, and it has a diversified, flexible global supply chain for raw materials. Third, she argued the company’s contracts have been designed for volatility; they include mechanisms such as pass-throughs and surcharges.

Company bullishness also comes through in its accelerated share repurchases. ATI bought back $70 million in the first quarter, and expects to buy back as much as $250 million worth of shares in the second quarter. In effect, this means buying up the annual allocation in just the first half.

I believe the outstanding sales, net income, and earnings growth in the first quarter are further validation of a medium moat.

ATI’s growth prospects

The company published guidance with this slide in the Q1 investor presentation:

To get more specific:

  • Adjusted EBITDA midpoint: $820 million, versus 2024’s actual of $729.1 million; this represents a year-over-year increase of 12.5%.
  • Adjusted EPS midpoint: $2.98 versus 2024’s actual of $2.46, means an increase of 21.1% (assuming it buys back $250 million worth of shares).
  • Free cash flow midpoint: $300 million versus 2024’s $247.6 million, another increase of 21.1%.

These targets, if achieved, would put ATI solidly back in the growth camp. Double-digit increases like these, coming on top of a share buyback, would be quite attractive to investors and likely lead to higher share prices.

Wall Street analysts are just as enthusiastic in their estimates of normalized EPS growth (these analyst estimates are normalized, while the company’s estimate is for adjusted EPS):

Over the next five years, these estimates provide an average annual EPS growth rate of 18.78%. To appreciate the magnitude of this sort of increase, consider the Rule of 72. If you divide 72 by the growth percentage, you get an estimate of how many years it would take to double your investment. In this case, it would take 3.83 years to double, which would be an excellent return.

That’s assuming the economy stays strong. ATI management believes the company can emerge relatively unscathed over tariffs, but these are questionable times. Would ATI be hurt if the economy drops into a recession or if, as some fear, there is a return of stagflation?

For those of us who experienced stagflation in the late 1970s and early 1980s, it is a worrying moment. Not only the stagflation itself, but also the cure, shock therapy, which saw interest rates soar above 20% and caused a short-term recession.

On the positive side of this issue, ATI had a backlog of $3.9 billion at the end of 2024, and for a company with $4.4 billion in sales last year, that’s an excellent backstop if business slows.

Valuation

Over the past decade, shareholders saw a capital gain of only 90.87%, but the past year alone has delivered a gain of 18.88%, driven mainly by the recent spike:

The spike began on April 23 when the price jumped by 10.52%, to $50.41, on news it had ratified a new collective bargaining agreement with some of its employees. The price continued to climb for all but one of the next 10 days, including a 14.45% boost on May 1. The latter date is when the Q1 earnings were released.

Between April 22, when the price closed at $45.61 and May 8 when it closed at $71.10, the price soared 55.89%. That’s an exceptional vote of confidence in the company by investors.

However, that burst of increases didn’t do anything positive for valuation ratios, as shown in this Seeking Alpha table:

While mathematically correct, I am treating the PEG GAAP TTM ratio of 10.59 as an outlier, a non-representative piece of data. That’s reinforced by the forward PEG ratio of 0.97, which is just below the dividing line between undervalued and overvalued.

To set a one-year target price, I’m going to use ATI’s adjusted EPS growth estimate of 21.1% for 2025 (despite my concern about the economy). Adding that percentage to the May 8 closing price of $71.10 gives us a target of $86.10.

That’s well above the 52-week high of $72.28 and the Wall Street analysts’ average estimate of $75.10, an increase of 5.69%.

Based on my price expectations, the effectiveness of management, and ATI’s robust prospects, I rate it a Buy. That would have been a Strong Buy if not for the threat of tariffs and fears of a recession.

One other Seeking Alpha analyst has posted a rating in the past 90 days, a Buy. The Quant system also has a Buy.

The Wall Street analysts have a collective Strong Buy, based on eight Strong Buys, two Buys, and one Hold. As for their revisions, they have six Up revisions and no Down revisions.

Risk factors

As expressed above several times, tariffs and their potential to cause major economic damage could be a significant threat. This no doubt reflects my experience of living through stagflation and the subsequent economic shock therapy, but the potential for damage is still very real.

ATI operates in highly competitive markets, and technologically sophisticated markets. If its R&D fails to keep up with competitors anxious to take away its market share, it may lose its profitability.

My assumptions, those of the company, and Wall Street analysts are based on a continuation of the past year or years. Conditions may turn out to be much different than they were in the past, making our assumptions incorrect.

Much of its recent success came as the aerospace industry rebounded from the weak, post-COVID demand for air travel and air freight, in an already cyclical industry. That growth may fade and revenues may fall.

In the 10-K for 2024 it reported “a range of increasing and evolving cybersecurity threats common to industrial operations and other enterprises, which continue to grow in frequency and scope.”

Conclusion

Aerospace and defense materials specialist ATI Inc. could enjoy double-digit earnings growth over the next several years. The biggest threat to that possibility comes from tariffs and the effect they may have on its American and international businesses.

The firm may be able to weather that storm, because of its American manufacturing base, its supply chain diversity, and a nearly $4 billion backlog.

Seeing potential earnings growth of more than 21% in 2025, I rate ATI a Buy.

data.title: What's The U.S. Dollar's Decline Telling Us?
data.description:
Transcript

Eric Winograd - One of the questions that’s come up a lot this year as the financial markets process the implications of the trade war is whether the dollar is either losing, or in danger of losing, its status as the reserve currency of the world.

And those questions really came to the fore in April because we saw the exchange value of the dollar go down, the price of treasury bonds go down — meaning the yields went up — and US equity markets go down, all at the same time. And that sort of simultaneous sell-off is pretty unusual. And it certainly looks, at least circumstantially, as though it might be attributable to reserve managers around the world reducing their dollar holdings and reducing their holdings of dollar-based assets.

I would caution here that we don’t get very good data on reserve flows in real time. So at best, this is a circumstantial case rather than one that we can prove. But it does force market participants to contemplate the scenario of the dollar losing stature around the world.

A big part of the reason the dollar has been the world’s reserve currency is because US economic policy has been predictable, has been process oriented, and has been rules-based. And right now, all three of those are sort of up in the air. And so it is clear, I think, that reserve managers around the world have an incentive to try to diversify away from the dollar.

For what it’s worth, I think that the US administration might welcome that, not because they want the dollar’s role in financial transactions or in the global economy to diminish but because a weaker dollar, which is what would likely result if reserve flows are reallocated, would help fulfill the administration’s objectives of reducing the US dollar’s trade deficit.

One of the clear implications if reserve flows reallocate is that interest rates are likely to be higher than they otherwise would be, for two reasons. One is that a weaker dollar would likely push inflation somewhat higher. But more significantly, large portions of dollar assets held by reserve managers are held in Treasuries. And if they were to reduce their holdings of Treasuries, that would also push interest rates higher.

The challenge, of course, is that it’s not clear what the alternative asset to the dollar is. There isn’t another currency that serves the same role in the global economy. Most other currencies are not as freely traded or are not as liquid. The economies on which they are based are not as large.

That makes us a little bit skeptical that we are seeing a dramatic reallocation of foreign exchange reserves. That said, it’s very possible we are seeing movement around the margins, and that changes in US policy are providing enough of an incentive to force reserve managers to look elsewhere. Certainly, we’ve seen appreciation in the euro that goes beyond what its cyclical fundamentals would dictate. There’s been a large increase in the price of gold.

We’re not ready to conclude yet that the dollar’s day in the sun is done, but it is something that we’ll have to monitor in the months going forward.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Win Streak Snapped
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 ended the week lower, falling 0.47% from last Friday and snapping its two-week win streak. The index is now sitting 7.88% below its record close from February 19th, 2025.

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached a then all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis.

It took over 5 years before the index reached a new then all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

Here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since May 1st and below the 200-day moving average since March 26th. Additionally, the 200-day moving average has been above the 50-day moving average since April 14th.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range.

On April 9th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 1.62%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently down 3.56% year to date, while the S&P Equal Weight is down 1.27% year to date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Helios Tower: Sound Fundamentals, Maintaining Buy
data.description:
Following the Q4 and Fiscal Year 2024 results, we are back to comment on Helios Towers plc (OTCPK:HTWSF). Our buy rating was supported by 1) positive growth prospects driven by colocations on existing towers providing EBITDA margin uplift, 2) a solid and resilient customer base, and 3) reduced M&A activity coupled with ongoing deleveraging, all confirming the company is on track in delivering its strategy. Since we initiated coverage (released at the end of January), A Leading Infrastructure Play To Buy, we have been up by >30%.

Q1 Results and Our Positive Take

Q1 2025 was a solid quarter for the company. Although sales missed consensus estimates by 5.21%, all other key metrics exceeded expectations. In number, Q1 turnover reached $203.8 million (+4.6% year-on-year) but missed Wall Street consensus by 5.21%. On a positive note, Helios Tower delivered tenancy growth, which was partially offset by lower power prices. The company delivered an adjusted EBITDA of $110.1 million (signing a plus +9.0% year-on-year) and was aligned with consensus expectations. More notably, the company exceeded its tenancy target, reaching 30,074 tenancies versus the projected 29,893.

Why are we still positive?

  1. This growth rate supports Helios Tower’s goal of achieving a tenancy ratio of 2.2x by 2026 (Fig. 1). Tenant growth was seen across standard and amended colocation agreements. Management noted during the earnings call that there is no economic difference between the two types of tenancies. Helios Towers’ initiatives to enhance FCF and return on invested capital showed meaningful progress. For this reason, the group reaffirmed its full-year 2025 outlook. Being an infrastructure player, the company has high fixed costs, leading to a high increase in ROIC for each extra tenant on the same tower (Fig. 2). This drives higher profitability, as adding tenants to existing towers significantly increases margins with limited additional CAPEX and OPEX costs;
  2. Still related to our positive investment equity story, the company’s credit profile improved, with S&P and Fitch upgrading Helios Towers from B+ to BB-, while Moody’s revised its outlook to B1 (Fig. 1). Supported by positive FCF (and lower M&A appetite), this reflects Helios Tower’s ongoing deleveraging efforts, as net leverage (net debt/annualised adj. EBITDA) improved year-over-year from 4.4x to 4.0x;
  3. Still related to Q1, 99% of top-line sales were derived from multinational mobile network operators. 67% of sales were in USD, the CFA Franc (pegged to the Euro), or the Omani Rial (pegged to the USD), reducing currency risk exposure;
  4. Helios Tower has a high backlog and long remaining customer contracts, at 6.9 years duration. It is also positive that one of the clauses in the contract highlights that customers cannot back out of the agreement once they sign the Master Services Agreement (MSA). The MSA is an agreement between Helios Tower and its telecom customers. The MSA allows the company to: 1) ensure predictable and recurring revenues, lowering the churn rate, 2) facilitate colocation, and 3) reduce risks to raise financing, as the future cash flows are locked. We believe this implication will lower its financing costs in the Helios Tower case.

Source: Helios Tower Q1 Results ppt - Fig 1

Fig 2

Valuation

Following Helios Tower Q1 results and given our 2025 projection, we confirmed sales of $850 million with a tenancy ratio of 2.1x (the ratio was 2.09x in Q1 2025 - Fig. 3). This should derive an adj. EBITDA of $465 million, implying a 55% EBITDA margin, which represents a 200 basis points step up above 2024 financials. Within our P&L projections, we have made a modest downward adjustment to D&A expenses, lowering them from $200 million to $190 million, due to reduced CAPEX (Fig. 3) and maintenance investments. Our outlook remains closely aligned with Helios Towers' 2025 guidance (Fig. 4). To recap, we continue to value the company using an EV/EBITDA approach, and, turning to the valuation, the stock trades at just 7.1x 2025 EBITDA. This represents a discount relative to peers, with an FWD EV/EBITDA of 8x (Fig. 5). We also feel this valuation is incorrect given Helios Tower's three-year EBITDA growth of 9%/17%/14% in FY22/FY23/FY24, respectively. With an FCF yield above 10%, we keep our EV/EBITDA valuation multiple of 8x unchanged. We forecasted an annual debt reduction of $100 million, which has already been achieved in the Q1 net debt evolution, so we feel Helios Tower's equity story is de-risked. With an enterprise value estimate of $3.7 billion and $1.7 million in net debt, our equity value reached $2 billion. Converting to British pounds, our target price is set at £142 per share. This implied a 28% upside potential.

Fig 3

Fig 4

Fig 5

Risks and Threats

We have covered the company twice this year, so we report the following downside risks: 1) clients concentration four customers are making up 74% of all revenues (Fig. 6), 2) lower colocation growth, 3) Telcos might build their towers and/or sector consolidation, 4) future technologies, such as Starlink, 5) there is an ongoing conflict in the Democratic Republic of Congo, this could affect business especially considering that it is one of Helios Tower biggest markets, 6) additional M&A with no deleverage path, 7) related to inflation, part of Heliow Tower sales growth came from CPI (Fig. 7), we questions how sustainable can be this in the future. Changes in tax and regulations might impact the company's valuation.

Fig 6

Fig 7

Conclusion

Helios Towers has sound fundamentals. In the past few years, its business has grown steadily while increasing margins. Considering its deleverage path, valuation discount compared to peers, and historical average, we continue to buy the company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Intesa Sanpaolo: High-Yield Dividends Fueled By Consistent Earnings Growth
data.description:
Introduction

Just a few years ago, banks were shunned as dangerous investments that resembled, to a certain extent, dead money. After all, the Great Financial Crisis and a zero-interest environment created two headwinds that undermined both their credibility and their earnings. This was particularly true in Europe, where a sovereign debt crisis was also felt. Then, as the economy rebounded after the pandemic, we flipped the page, and we saw for the first time in over a decade, interest rates rising aggressively to tame inflation.

Well, among the best European banks we find Intesa Sanpaolo (OTCPK:ISNPY; OTCPK:IITSF), which is currently one of the largest European banks by market cap. Currently, it is my largest holding, although it was not so a few years ago when I invested in it as the stock plunged during the 2022 bear market. But since it has gone up 150% on my average cost, it is now a 12% position. In this article, I would like to review its latest Q1 2025 earnings and explain what I am doing with my position and why.

Intesa Sanpaolo: A Quick Overview

First of all, Intesa Sanpaolo is the largest banking group in Italy and currently the third one by market cap in Europe (EUR 84.5B), making it the most valuable company listed on the Euronext Milan Stock Exchange. It has a weight of almost 11% on the FTSE MIB index, followed by its peer UniCredit (market cap of EUR 81.3B) and the largest European utility Enel (EUR 78.6B). In fourth place, we find Ferrari (EUR 74.2B). These four make up 40% of the whole index.

Now, the Italian banking landscape is currently quite interesting as a big consolidation Risk, as it is often called in the news, is taking place, with larger and smaller banks launching public offerings to acquire other peers. It would be very interesting to explain it, and I plan on writing an article on this intriguing matter. But, for what pertains to the purpose of this article, we should know that Intesa has called itself out of this and has repeatedly stated that it has the size it wants to have with the right mix of business branches. As a result, it may be less speculative, but it also offers more stability.

However, the most important reason I picked Intesa and am bullish on it is linked to a big and important macrotrend I see in Western economies, and, in particular, in the Italian one: wealth management. Italians are renowned for being good savers (the personal savings rate is usually above 10%; in the U.S., it is around 5%), and Italian households hold around $1.3T in savings accounts. This huge pile of money needs to be managed, and in recent years, many hot inflation prints have raised a new awareness in the retail public. As a result, wealth management banks are seeing a greater and greater influx of billions. Well, Intesa Sanpaolo is the largest wealth management bank in Italy. The bank has indirect deposits of EUR 796B, of which EUR 470B of which are assets under management.

Furthermore, Italian corporates are currently better capitalized than in the past and have very high liquidity buffers. This situation shields Intesa from unexpected losses on loans or other corporate services.

It is also one of the most efficient banks in Europe, with a cost/income ratio of 38% (best in class) that helps its return on capital be rather high (ROE of 19.6% and ROTE of 23.95%)

In terms of safety, it has a CET 1 ratio of 13%, a total capital ratio of 18.% and a leverage ratio of 5.7%. These are all quite conservative metrics that show Intesa is not inclined to be too speculative.

Intesa Sanpaolo Q1 2025 Earnings

Let's take a quick look at the quarterly development of the income statement. This is particularly important because it allows us to monitor the behavior of net interest income, which is strictly related to interest rates. Although the ECB is lowering interest rates (2.25% as of April 2025), we see that the bank is holding up quite well and was able to report EUR 3.6B in net interest income.

Moreover, net income grew thanks to lower operating costs and taxes. Intesa Sanpaolo has made it clear that it wants to reduce personnel expenses (3,000 headcount reduction achieved in Q1) to become leaner and more efficient. Well, it is delivering on target.

I said Intesa interests me because of its profile as an investment bank and an asset manager. Investment banking did particularly well this quarter, with its operating income posting a 21.7% YoY increase to EUR 1.23B. Asset management was around flat at EUR 239M (Q1 2024: EUR 240M).

When we consider wealth management, it contributed to around 42% of gross income, with commission increasing 11% and a 38% growth of the financial assets managed through a 360-degree service.

Intesa has fully de-risked its balance sheet, achieving the zero-NPL bank status (net NPL ratio of only 1%).

Now, let's go back to Intesa's net income of EUR 2.6B, which is a record-high, as we can see from the graph below, with EPS growing at an even faster pace (17%), thanks to share repurchases. This result gives confidence to the bank to forecast an annual ROTE of 24% and an annual ROE of 20%. Therefore, the bank confirmed that this year it should earn EUR 9B in net income. This is particularly important because Intesa links its distribution to its net income, usually paying 70% of it. With these projections, we are talking about an enticing dividend yield of around 8%. In Q1, EUR 1.8B cash dividends have already been accrued.

Intesa also confirmed it will pay in May the second tranche of its FY24 dividend that will be worth EUR 3B. Furthermore, a buyback program worth EUR 2B will be launched in June. This could be particularly effective because in late May the stock will pay its dividend and, therefore, the stock price will move down a bit.

As Intesa's CEO Carlo Messina explained during the earnings call:

Intesa Sanpaolo offers one of the highest dividend yields in European banking. And this year, we will return at least €8.2 billion, taking into account the May dividend, the June buyback, and the interim dividend in November. Additional capital distribution will be quantified at the end of the year.

Of course, a bank is much more than its dividends. But when a bank consistently raises its dividend, adding share repurchases, fueling these returns with consistent earnings growth, then we have a very solid company right in front of us.

Intesa Sanpaolo's Valuation

Let's now ask ourselves if Intesa deserves its current market cap and its valuation.

Currently, the bank trades at a fwd PE of 10, fwd EV/EBITDA of 11. But with banks, we know that we should consider the price-to-book ratio. Well, the fwd P/B ratio is 1.09, more or less in line with its French peer BNP Paribas (OTCQX:BNPQY). Banco Santander (SAN) trades at a 1.2, while Unicredit (OTCPK:UNCRY) trades at a higher P/B of around 1.4; the same is true for the Spanish BBVA.

Considering a dividend yield around 8% and an upcoming buyback program worth another 2.4%, we have a bank whose valuation is still attractive, and this is why it earns an A as its valuation grade.

I think investors have a double choice here. In two weeks, Intesa will pay its semiannual dividend. On one side, investors could buy right now to receive this dividend. On the other side, they could wait for the dividend to be paid to buy the stock at a cheaper price without having to pay withholding taxes (26% in Italy). In any case, Intesa is set to deliver strong results through this year and the next. As a result, I keep rating it a convincing buy after its Q1 2025 report.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ares Capital: Stalwart BDC Keeps Rolling Along With A Solid Q1 Result
data.description:
In this article, we discuss the latest quarterly results from the Business Development Company Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC). The company delivered a solid 2.1% total NAV return over Q1. We last covered the stock here.

ARCC trades at a 9.4% dividend yield and has a core net investment income yield of 9.8%. Its price is at a 3% premium to book, about 16% above the median valuation in our coverage.

The portfolio sector allocation is fairly typical of BDCs with software and healthcare being the key areas. It has a lower first-lien portfolio than the average BDC with a focus on larger companies than the traditional middle-market BDC borrowers.

Quarter Update

Core net investment income came in at $0.50 - a drop of $0.05 versus the previous quarter. GAAP NII held relatively steady at $0.54 or only a penny below the previous quarter due to a relatively high 4 cents of capital gains incentive fee accruals. The key driver of NII outside of volatile incentive fees was the lagged pass-through of the last Fed rate cut. NII should stabilize from here until the Fed decides to make its next move.

Oddly, the market expected NII of $0.54 or only a penny lower from Q4 despite the fact that there was a non-recurring $0.01 special dividend from the JV Ivy Hill, i.e. the market, in effect, expected a flat NII. This disappointment caused the stock to fall nearly 3% on the day.

The dividend remained flat at $0.48 - it has been flat since 2022. Dividend coverage fell to 104%. The spillover is $1.29 per share. Unless the Fed delivers on its 4 rate cuts as the market expects, the company is unlikely to have to cut the dividend in our view.

This was the first quarterly NAV drop since the end of 2022. With less retained income due to Fed rate cuts, there is less of an upward bias to the NAV. A low level of M&A activity will also likely weigh on fee-related earnings as well.

The impact of tariffs is the key question for the sector. Management said that there is only a small number of borrowers, comprising a mid-single-digit share, that is directly exposed to potential impacts from tariffs via imports. This could work out to a double-digit exposure figure on NAV, so this issue is worth watching. All in all, it's too early to tell what the impact will be, and tariff levels could easily fall from current levels.

Income Dynamics

Net new investments were barely above zero.

As a result of this and additional share issuance, leverage ticked lower to the second-lowest level over the past couple of years and towards the bottom of the company's target range. Leverage has been on a downtrend over the past couple of years, which makes sense given lending spreads have mostly trended down as well. This trend could reverse in Q2 as spreads have risen, and the company may decide to take advantage of this to boost lending, particularly as banks appear to have cooled their lending activity recently amid market volatility.

Asset yield fell slightly as the last Fed rate cut worked its way through, while interest expense held steady at a below-sector level of 4.91%.

At the start of the year, the company issued $1bn of 7-year unsecured notes at a swapped level of SOFR + 1.7% which is on the lower end of its financing and below the cost of its credit facilities. This likely allowed it to reduce the spread on its BNP funding facilities by 0.2% to +1.9%, a small tailwind to NII.

At this point, the pass-through from the Fed's rate cuts is done, and the base case is for NII to be roughly flat next quarter and in excess of the dividend. One risk is that the company doesn't see enough attractive lending opportunities, causing its leverage to keep falling, further reducing NII. At the moment, this seems unlikely, particularly as the cooling of the bank leverage loan market should steer more opportunities towards direct lending.

The drop in interest expense costs will help support NII through the rest of the year. Widening spreads should also be a tailwind for NII as new deals are already higher in yield by 0.25-0.5% with fee levels higher as well.

If the Fed delivers on 4 rate cuts by the end of the year as the market expects, the company's NII will likely test its dividend of $0.48 which may require the company to dip into its sizable spillover.

Portfolio Quality

Non-accruals fell slightly to 0.9% - below the median in our coverage - though they remain above the level of most of 2024.

Portfolio quality, as gauged by internal ratings, was stable.

PIK fell and remains near the median in our coverage.

There was a net realized loss, though not exceptional by historical standards.

The first-lien allocation continued to increase, which is good to see, however, it remains well below the sector average level.

The weighted-average loan-to-value of the portfolio is in the low 40%s while the weighted-average debt-to-EBITDA of borrower companies is the lowest since Q1-2020. Interest coverage is close to 2x. All in all, portfolio risk metrics remain stable.

Return And Valuation Profile

ARCC continues to perform well. The return in Q1 was on the low side, but still managed a 10% annualized level. There was a period of 3 quarters in 2022 where the quarterly return was between 1-2%, so if things worsen somewhat we could see performance fall back towards that level. The peak high-yield corporate bond spread level in 2022 hit 6%, and it is now 3.7% so we are quite a way off that level of stress.

The company's current valuation of a 3% premium is near its historical median level.

However, the valuation differential to the sector average valuation is very elevated. Arguably, this is well-deserved given the company's sustained level of outperformance.

On a valuation-adjusted basis, its 3-year performance is in the top half of our coverage universe.

Stance And Takeaways

ARCC remains a stalwart of the BDC sector not only because of its size but also because of its strong performance and not overly excessive valuation. The company's history of keeping its head above water during recessions and periods of market stress means there is always a strong argument for an allocation. Its portfolio is the most diversified of any public BDC which means a single problem is little more than a rounding error. For now, however, we view the stock's relative valuation to the sector median as somewhat rich in relative terms, so we wait for a better entry point.
